title: Seville orange marmalade
subtitle: The classic bitter-orange preserve, made in January.
excerpt: Seville oranges simmered to extract pectin, sliced peel cooked until tender in the juice and water, then set to a thick, bittersweet marmalade with granulated sugar. The definitive winter preserve. Makes about 6 jars.
sourceNotes: Seville orange marmalade. The name derives from the Portuguese 'marmelada' (quince paste). The association with bitter Seville oranges and the British breakfast table was established in the 18th and 19th centuries. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) contains a foundational recipe. Seville oranges are only available in the UK in January and early February.

recipe.temperatureNote: Setting point is 104.5°C on a sugar thermometer.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a jam or marmalade will set as it cools. For most preserves, this is 104.5°C. Test with a cold saucer: a drop of jam pushed with a finger should wrinkle.

content.text: Seville oranges are only available in January, and marmalade season lasts about six weeks. The bitter Seville variety has a high pectin and acid content that gives a firmer set and a more complex flavour than sweet oranges. Buy extra when you see them; the pips and pith are where the pectin lives, and both go into the muslin bag for extraction.

Halve {{seville-orange}} of Seville oranges and squeeze out the juice. Reserve the pips and any loose pith. Scrape the pith from the orange halves with a spoon; tie the pith, pips and squeezed halves in a piece of muslin. Slice the peel thinly: the thinner the slice, the finer the marmalade.

Put the sliced peel, muslin bag, {{water}} of water, the orange juice and the juice of {{lemon}} lemons in a large jam pan. Bring to the boil, then simmer gently for 1 to 1.5 hours until the peel is completely soft. Squeeze the muslin bag against the side of the pan to extract as much pectin-rich liquid as possible; discard the bag.
Add sugar and boil to setting point

Add {{granulated-sugar}} of warmed sugar and stir over low heat until dissolved. Bring to a full rolling boil and boil hard, without stirring, for 15 to 20 minutes. Test with a cold saucer: a drop of marmalade that wrinkles when pushed with a finger has reached setting point. Or use a thermometer: 104.5°C. Leave to cool for 10 minutes in the pan, then stir gently to distribute the peel and ladle into sterilised jars.

items.cause: Under-cooked; did not reach setting point.
items.fix: Return to the boil and cook for 5 more minutes; test again. A marmalade that is still liquid after 24 hours can be re-boiled.

items.cause: Sugar added before peel was fully soft.
items.fix: The peel must be completely soft before the sugar goes in; sugar fixes the texture permanently. Check by squeezing a piece; it should offer no resistance.

items.symptom: Peel floating to the top of the jars
items.cause: Jarred too hot, without the resting period.
items.fix: Leave the marmalade to cool in the pan for 10 minutes and stir gently before jarring; this redistributes the peel more evenly.

content.text: Seville orange marmalade is the preserve of the British winter: available only in January, made in a weekend afternoon, eaten for months on toast. It belongs to a specific tradition of seasonal British preserving that takes one narrow window when an ingredient is at its best and converts it into something that lasts all year. A pot of homemade marmalade is among the most satisfying things a kitchen can produce.
